item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image comes from here and is copyrighted and the second image's original source has been lost, with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this but I feel like the Ethics Committee would come down hard on further testing and most likely reprimand Dr. Simon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a good idea, I'll keep it in mind with my own writing.

item.title: Regarding the recent page revision
item.content:encoded: Just an FYI, I went ahead and changed the audio player from Flash to HTML5, as Flash is rapidly losing support and already can't be viewed by many people, including Firefox and Mobile users. No content changes were made, and the article is otherwise unchanged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's something I was thinking about too. I have no sympathy for Decard and feel like he's kind of a karma Houdini. Not that there's any shortage of those on this site. but it's still an awesome and terrifying scip

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any guesses how many of the 137 people sent to hell were unjustly convicted?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user is permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's just say that hate and grief are a currency to some. When I was willing to pay the price, help found me.

Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Magnificent. Reminds me of the Beyond from the Night's Dawn trilogy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm just imagining Decard in the empty building, just humming to himself at a job well done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes.

Yes, this is sort of scary.

And such.

gotta change my pants now k bai

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: May be your first SCP, but I feel like it's your last also. I had a peek in your sandbox and your 'old smelly dog' skip seems promising so far. C'mon, feed us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the heads up! I'll definitely check it out. I spent enough time perusing creepy photographs of the place that it's firmly fixed in my imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing alone gets an upvote from me…

But The fact that you scored a shot of Eastern State Penitentiary (Which is a scary fucking old prison, anomalies or not) sings to my heart, especially since I just happened to have been watching episodes of a show that was on MTV, aptly called "MTV's Fear," and they featured that prison as one of the places. (One guy had to sit in a cell for 18 hours) And yes, supposedly it's haunted. Watch the episode on Youtube, it's kind of funny in a dark way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I look forward to seeing more SCPs from you in the future. This is phenomenal, especially for a first attempt.

I noticed how one of the release dates is in the 3000s, which by my estimations would be around 1000000000 years of sensory deprivation and the general case of being stuck.

It's horrible enough to go through 30 days (15 mins) of that and most people would be driven mad by then, and a billion years seem daunting at first but something else might happen instead.

In the most boring case scenario, the person would return a vegetable. Or perhaps the person's mind, after going through the inevitable madness state after 30 days, would instead 'evolve' to cope with the boredom of the billion year wait. The mind might end up creating an elaborate hallucination for the person to live in. Somebody might be living a normal life oblivious to the fact that they are actually just chilling in a void for eternity, Matrix-style.

Perhaps when 3300 comes about and the prisoner is finally released, it would be the matrix all over again. Or perhaps the mind, instead of merely hallucinating, would evolve to a higher state of being and what would materialize in a thousand years from now would not even be human anymore. Just cause it's immortal doesn't mean that it can't 'evolve' in other ways.

I'm just bullcrapping here although due to the mysteries of the mind, who knows what would happened if you leave a consciousness in a void for aeons. Oh and very good article BTW, very impressive for a first SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very impressive. I especially liked the interview with Decard.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the concept and the photograph as well. Bravo on your skip +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Utterly, utterly horrifying. Superb. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was initially put of, because the concept wasn't particularly original. But you placed a new twist on it.

I really liked the parallel between what happened inside the cell and what the guard did to the prisoners before taking them to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get what you were going for but a list of phobias doesn't make me feel anything. Instead of just naming pathologies, I would rather you describe the actual symptoms, which is both more precise and more horrifying. It's the difference between:

  • "haphephobia" (does nothing for me, plus I had to Google it)
  • vs "fear of being touched" (more descriptive but still rather bland)
  • vs "subjects begin screaming uncontrollably when medical staff attempt physical examination" (ok! now I'm getting chills)

You can often convey what a person is feeling by describing how they react to things. A guy who tries to claw out his eyes while screaming "TURN OFF! THE LIGHTS!" is probably suffering from some photophobia, no clinical language needed.

Thanks for writing. It's not a waste of time at all or I wouldn't bother commenting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like Pig_catapult said, the critical aspect of the cell is not that it makes you crazy (even though I put a fair amount of words into saying exactly that) but how it does so. There's no compulsion or insanity ray, it's just the inevitable outcome of a lifetime's worth of total isolation.

I found it very difficult to convey the experience of absence and void in clinical language, which is why I tried my best to use the interview to nudge the reader to really consider what being a disembodied consciousness would be like. The list of phobias is just meant to emphasize that subjects were left terrified of basic stimuli because they could no longer rationally process the sensations.

Regardless, thank you for taking the time to critique the work. I hope it at least didn't feel like a waste of your time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe this uses images from the same location as SCP-450; not that there's anything wrong with that.
Good job for your first one! May you make many more of this quality!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm thoroughly impressed. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even just regular, completely mundane solitary confinement is proven to cause extensive neurological and psychological damage in humans. My assumption from the start was that all it is is the total sensory deprivation, and you go mad all on your own because that's what human brains do in those circumstances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*long string of excited squeeing noises*

I didn't even realise this was your first skip. This makes me, like, triple happy now. Congrats. =D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP attempt

Where do these people come from?

Amazing first attempt, I love the narrative in this. Also appreciating the 666+1 cell number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's a horrible person, no question, but goddamn the balls he must have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What really does it for me, is what Samuel Decard did. Sending the guards home, starving the prisoners, taking each one out himself and putting them into the cell. It's brutal.

You'd imagine that between the prisoners being violent criminals and them realizing no one was coming back that they'd give him one hell of a fight, yet he managed to do it anyways. Makes for one scary sonuvabitch in my mind. He got tagged and had to stack the odds in his favor, but he dragged each and every one of them down to that cell on his own.

I could almost care less about the cell itself, but that man, Samuel Decard, has my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Objectively well-done, with an extra +1 for Philadelphia content. (Eastern State is already scary, even before you add the sci-fi elements).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the record, I first saw your draft when you put it on reddit and my thought was "Holy crap, this is really well written for a first scip. I should make sure mine is as fun to read."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Knocks my first SCP out of the park man, bravo! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, Kalinin. I didn't keep a written record of who I talked to each time so I wasn't able to give proper thanks (note for next time). You were the one that first pointed out The Jaunt to me as well, right?

In any case, I definitely took your earlier criticism to heart and I made a number of changes to the draft to try and address the concerns. I think the scip is a *lot* better for having had your input.
- Lowered the time frame of the coverup from X number of years to ~4 months
- Cut the number of prisoners to 1/4 of what it was initially
- Made it explicit that he was actively bribing inquirers that he couldn't delay otherwise
- Made it explicit that he put the entire place into lockdown before moving people.
- Had him do nearly all of the moving after he had starved the prisoners.

I realize that there is an inherent "ask" of the reader about the plausibility of the character doing it all alone, but when I wrote up a version where he had help, I thought it hurt the story even more.

I wish I could find a way to write it in a way that let you suspend disbelief. I genuinely tried to give sufficient rationale to justify the story I wanted to tell, but it appears I didn't succeed in your case. Hopefully, my next attempt will be more up your alley.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. Brings a lot together, fairly well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This matches my conception of Hell… alone in the dark with nothing but your own thoughts. Forever. Strong +1 for this and for the interviews. Great first effort!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said in the draft thread, it leaves me asking what's the point. The effect is sensory deprivation until you go crazy to death. Is it just a torture device? If so, why does anybody come out at all? Did whatever helped build it have any motivation other than to make the hoo-mans go crazy?

Those phobias read like a shopping list of crazy that you took down to the crazy store. Honey, they're all out of astraphobia, should we get acousticophobia instead? Also the phrases "mental trauma", "breaks down rational thought structures", "cognitive shutdown", "psychological breakdown", and "violent insanity" are all just non-clinical synonyms for crazy. Okay, they go crazy, I understand. It would be more horrifying to read a description of specific symptoms than just a grab-bag of words all meaning crazy.

Good idea, good picture, but ultimately didn't take me anywhere.

item.title: Re: wow
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure which redaction you're referring to, could you be more specific? I thought I'd pulled the last of the [REDACTED]s after Musuko suggested a great way to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As mentioned in the chat, I can't get past the complete absurdity of one man running a state prison a) completely by himself with no one at all noticing for that length of time and b) without being shanked the second the inmates find out there's only one person guarding the place.

I'm ambivalent about the concept, but the immersion-breaking lack of any sort of rationality here pushes me solidly into downvote territory.

item.title: wow
item.content:encoded:
For a first SCP that was amazing. I was intrigued and hooked throughout the entire read. Please make more in the future. Although i don't know why that one little bit was redacted, right after the confined wind part. It's just a nitpick though.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how the release is exactly at midnight, but the researchers were able to figure out how to do durations less than a day (put them in at 11:45 for a 15 minute trip, etc). In some of the worse writing I've seen you would think researchers were complete idiots who wouldn't figure that out.
If you do more testing logs, I would be curious to see:

  • What happens if the release date is in the past
  • What happens if you put someone's name on the list who isn't in the area. (Maybe they're in another prison, or they are just missing).
  • Putting incorrect or weird stuff on the paper. Name of someone other than the guy in the cell, writing the crime down instead of release date.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's your first attempt?

Good heavens. I expect similar work from you in the future. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do I want to say that's Emily's voice on the recording?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sensory deprivation with time dilation? Vengeful prison administrator with eldritch support? Great writing with a good picture to boot?

This tickles me in all the right places. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely incredible, I loved it. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice one! A definite strong +1 from me now.

Excellently put together, delivered with plenty of attention to technical details (which always help make an SCP feel real), and a sublime conclusion with Decard's final sentence making me wonder who the mysterious help was.

Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That does the trick for me. Thank you Musuko!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a suitable explanation. How about putting something like the below to make it explicit?

Something as simple as the below might well do it:

Researchers observing an event have recorded audio of confined wind and additional anomalous sounds (see Experimental Trial 2701-13 Audio Log) emanating from within the cell immediately before activation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's come up in several other critiques, so I'm starting to think I might just have to let it go.

My thought was that since I provide an actual listenable example of the sound, "artistic absence" leaves interpretation open to the reader. I have had people hear crackling, quiet screaming and whispering all from the same sound file. I took that as a good sign.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lovely SCP, and an amazing first one at that.

However…

confined wind and [REDACTED] from within the cell immediately before activation

Unless you can give a convincing reason for why that is redacted, I can't give this an upvote yet. It's a great article. PLEASE don't let it be marred by what to me comes across as a lazy redaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I chose it because it is a semiconductor with some weird quantum properties when in nanoparticle form.

One thing that sometimes irks me in SCPs is that *nothing* odd is found about them, or that they are described as being impenetrable to analysis. It's a personal preference to have the Foundation see that some things are weird in terms of construction/composition but not understand how those properties lead to the anomalous effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My god the time frames involved. Someone got a release date of Christmas 3000?
Chilling. A good articulation of hell
+1
Why the cadmium selenide?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP attempt

Thanks to those who provided editing and critique: Annalea, Voct, EdAWACSdenyY, Jeseral, Devereux, azzleflux, Kalinin, Pig_catapult, Musuko and anyone I might have missed whilst asking in #site19.
